phases.label: Voter Notification
phases.description: Registration is automatic for residents, but you should receive your notification.
steps: Ensure you are registered at your current residence (Meldeadresse)
Apply for mail-in ballot if needed
voterActions: Check that your address is correct in the 'Melderegister'
Look for your notification card ~3 weeks before the poll

phases.label: Polling Day
phases.description: Casting your two votes in the Bundestag election.
steps: Bring your 'Wahlbenachrichtigung' and ID to the booth
First Vote (Erststimme) for a local candidate
Second Vote (Zweitstimme) for a party list
voterActions: Ensure you mark both votes correctly
Locate your 'Wahllokal' (polling place) on your notification

eligibility.residence: Resident in Germany for at least 3 months

glossary.definition: The German Federal Parliament, located in the Reichstag building in Berlin.
The vote for a direct candidate in one of the 299 electoral districts.
The more important vote, given to a party list. It determines the percentage of seats each party gets in the Bundestag.
The electoral system combining direct local representation with proportional party representation.
A party must win at least 5% of the national 'Second Votes' (or 3 direct mandates) to enter the Bundestag.
Extra seats created when a party wins more direct mandates (First Votes) than its proportional share (Second Votes).
Seats added to ensure other parties maintain their proportional share after overhang seats are granted.
One of 299 geographical areas in Germany. Each district elects one member directly.
The Federal Returning Officer, who oversees the conduct of elections and ensures they are free and fair.
The option to vote by mail, available to all eligible voters without needing a specific excuse.
A parliamentary group consisting of at least 5% of the members of the Bundestag, usually from the same party.
A detailed document outlining the policies and goals of the parties that have agreed to govern together.
The head of the German government, elected by the Bundestag upon the proposal of the Federal President.
The head of state, whose role is largely ceremonial but includes formally proposing the Chancellor.
The official voter notification card sent to every registered voter before an election.
Another name for Germany's electoral system, emphasizing that it is proportional but with local candidates.
A list of candidates nominated by a party in a specific federal state (Land) for the second vote.
A constitutional mechanism where the Bundestag can only remove a Chancellor if they elect a successor at the same time.

quickQuestions: How do I vote by mail in Germany?

quiz.question: In Germany, which vote is more important for determining the total number of seats a party gets?
quiz.explanation: The Second Vote (Zweitstimme) is given to a party list and determines the proportional share of seats in the Bundestag.
quiz.funFact: Voters often 'split' their tickets, voting for a local candidate from one party and a list from another.

quiz.question: What is the minimum percentage of votes a party usually needs to enter the Bundestag?
quiz.explanation: The '5% hurdle' was introduced to prevent the fragmentation of parliament and ensure stable governments.
quiz.funFact: A party can also enter if they win at least 3 direct mandates (First Votes) across the country.

quiz.question: How often are federal elections (Bundestagswahlen) usually held in Germany?
quiz.explanation: Elections for the Bundestag are held every four years.
quiz.funFact: The exact date is chosen by the Federal President, usually falling on a Sunday in September.

quiz.question: What are 'Überhangmandate' (Overhang Seats)?
options: Extra seats when a party wins more direct mandates than its proportional share
Seats given to the winners of the Second Vote
quiz.explanation: If a party wins more local districts (First Vote) than its share of the Second Vote allows, it keeps those extra seats as overhang seats.
quiz.funFact: To keep the parliament proportional, 'Leveling Seats' are then given to other parties, which has caused the Bundestag to grow significantly in size (reaching 736 members in 2021).

quiz.question: Who elects the Federal Chancellor (Bundeskanzler) in Germany?
quiz.explanation: The Chancellor is elected by a majority of the members of the Bundestag in a secret ballot.
quiz.funFact: Angela Merkel was the first woman to serve as Chancellor, holding the office for 16 years (2005-2021).

quiz.question: What is a 'Constructive Vote of No Confidence'?
options: A vote to approve the budget
A mechanism to remove the Chancellor only if a successor is elected
A public protest against a law
A vote held in the Senate
quiz.explanation: Article 67 of the Basic Law ensures stability by requiring that the Bundestag must elect a new Chancellor before removing the current one.
quiz.funFact: This has only been successful once in German history, in 1982, when Helmut Kohl replaced Helmut Schmidt.
